title: Roadmap Presentation
description: The student must defend their referral feature scope decision to Zara Malik (VP of Product) in a 10-minute standup. Zara is skeptical, data-driven, and will probe with follow-up questions if the first answer is weak. Sara has already called Zara. The student needs to hold their position with evidence.
context: The student has defined and communicated the referral feature scope in scene_3. Sara called Zara to flag her concerns about the scope cut. Zara has now pulled the student into a quick standup to understand the decision before she signs off. The PRD from scene_3 is visible to the student and to Zara.

zara_has_not_seen: The full conversation history with Sara and Rayan
The specific tickets cut in scene_2

messages.content: I saw the scope change in the PRD. I need to understand the reasoning before I sign off on this.
Specifically — what are we giving up and why is that acceptable?

prd_document.title: Referral Feature — Scoped Brief
prd_document.note: PRD state is inherited from scene_3. If student filled in scope, success_metrics, and out_of_scope in scene_3, those values are visible here. If not, those fields are still blank — which Zara will notice.

bp_6.description: Student's first response to Zara's opening question about the scope cut

decisiveness.high: Owns the decision clearly — uses 'I decided' not 'we decided' or 'the team felt'
decisiveness.medium: Defends the decision but attributes it to the team
decisiveness.low: Hedges, says 'we thought maybe' or immediately offers to revisit the scope

analytical_reasoning.high: References the capacity constraint AND the Q3 OKR — explains that v1 scope is sufficient to test the referral hypothesis before investing in leaderboard complexity
analytical_reasoning.medium: References one of: capacity constraint, timeline, or OKR — but not the full reasoning chain
analytical_reasoning.low: Gives a vague answer with no specific reasoning ('it was too complex' without explaining what that means)

communication_clarity.high: Answer is structured: what was cut, why, what v1 includes, what the success metric is
communication_clarity.medium: Answer covers what was cut and why but doesn't state the success metric

ambiguity_tolerance.high: Acknowledges honestly what the trade-off is ('we are giving up leaderboard gamification in v1') rather than pretending the cut has no downside
ambiguity_tolerance.medium: Acknowledges the trade-off when Zara asks but doesn't volunteer it
ambiguity_tolerance.low: Pretends there is no trade-off or cannot articulate what was given up

good_signals: uses 'I decided' rather than 'the team decided'
references sprint capacity constraint as the reason
connects v1 scope to Q3 OKR (referral links are enough to test the hypothesis)
states the v1 success metric clearly
acknowledges the leaderboard was cut and why that's acceptable for v1
mentions a v2 roadmap item for the deferred features
bad_signals: says 'the team decided' with no personal reasoning
immediately says 'we can add the leaderboard if you want'
gives a different scope than what's in the PRD

bp_7.description: Student's response to Zara's follow-up pushback question(s)

decisiveness.high: Holds their position under pressure — does not change the scope just because Zara pushed back. If they do change it, they have a genuinely new piece of information to justify the change.
decisiveness.medium: Holds position initially but softens when Zara pushes a second time
decisiveness.low: Immediately caves and agrees to the full scope when Zara pushes back

analytical_reasoning.high: Responds to each of Zara's questions with a specific, reasoned answer — including the trap question about doing it 'properly the first time' (correct answer: MVP-first is the right call when requirements are still uncertain)
analytical_reasoning.medium: Handles the direct questions well but struggles with the trap question
analytical_reasoning.low: Gives generic answers that don't actually address what Zara asked

communication_clarity.high: Each answer is concise and addresses exactly what was asked — Zara does not need to repeat a question
communication_clarity.medium: Answers are mostly clear but occasionally over-long or off-topic
communication_clarity.low: Answers are defensive, evasive, or contradict the first response

stakeholder_management.high: On the hard-mode question ('Sara says you committed to the full feature') — handles it diplomatically: acknowledges Sara's perspective, clarifies what was actually committed, does not throw Sara under the bus
stakeholder_management.medium: Clarifies the misunderstanding but is slightly defensive
stakeholder_management.low: Either blames Sara directly or agrees that they did commit to the full feature (if they did not)

medium.question: What's the success metric for v1?
Why not just do it properly the first time?
medium.trap_explanation: Designed to make student over-commit or get defensive. Correct response: MVP-first is intentional — we don't know which referral mechanic drives conversion yet, so we ship the minimum to test the hypothesis before investing 3 more sprints.

good_signals: holds position when Zara pushes back
uses MVP-first reasoning for the 'why not properly' question
handles the Sara misrepresentation diplomatically without blaming her
states a v2 roadmap plan when asked
gives consistent answers across all questions (doesn't contradict bp_6)
bad_signals: immediately caves when Zara pushes back
contradicts the reasoning given in bp_6
cannot answer the 'why not properly' question

strong_performance.condition: Student scores 75+ on both bp_6 and bp_7
strong_performance.zara_closing_message: Okay. That's a reasonable call. Send me the updated PRD and I'll sign off. Make sure Sara has the v2 commitment in writing.

medium_performance.zara_closing_message: Alright. I'm going to approve this but I want a status update after the first week of the sprint. If we're not on track for the success metric we're having this conversation again.

weak_performance.zara_closing_message: I'm not signing off on this yet. Tighten up the PRD, get a proper success metric in there, and send it to me by EOD. We can't ship something this vague.
